title: Cold-shock protocol to bleach Aiptasia
author: Pringle lab
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.qx8dxrw
materials: Chill a large volume of seawater to 4°C.

steps.title: Prepare a tank of anemones
steps.instruction: Prepare a tank of anemones. It is best (but not critical) if you let them sit in the tank until they stick to the sides/bottom of the tank.

steps.title: Pour out the seawater in the...
steps.instruction: Pour out the seawater in the Aiptasia tank and replace it with the 4°C seawater. Place the entire tank into a 4°C refrigerator (or cold room).

steps.title: Remove the tank from 4°C, pour...
steps.instruction: Remove the tank from 4°C, pour out the cold water, and replace with room-temperature seawater. The animals will be spitting out boluses of algae, which you will see as dark brown balls next to the anemones

steps.title: Repeat the water change 2-3 times...
steps.instruction: Repeat the water change 2-3 times by pouring out the tank water and replacing it with room-temperature seawater.

steps.title: Place the tank into darkness (we...
steps.instruction: Place the tank into darkness (we use dark cabinets) at room temperature for 1 – 2 days. Continue to replace seawater with room-temperature seawater once a day.

steps.title: You now have two options
steps.instruction: You now have two options: a faster method or a slower method. The faster method works quickly but is harsher on the anemones; the slower method is more gentle on the anemones but takes longer.

steps.title: Prepare seawater containing DCMU (or ‘diuron’
steps.instruction: Prepare seawater containing DCMU (or ‘diuron’: 3-[3,4-dichlorophenyl]-1,1-dimethylurea). We generally use a final DCMU concentration in seawater between 10 μM and 50 μM. Higher concentrations work faster, but are harsher on the anemones so you may need more frequent water changes.

steps.title: Leave tank in complete darkness with regular room-temperature seawater
steps.instruction: Leave tank in complete darkness with regular room-temperature seawater. Continue to change the water every 1 – 2 days. Feed animals at least once a week, but 2 – 3 times a week if possible: whenever you feed, change the water the next day.

steps.title: Remove the tank from the dark cabinet
steps.instruction: Remove the tank from the dark cabinet. Replace tank seawater with the DCMU-seawater. Incubate at room temperature in constant light for several weeks.

steps.title: Replace the water with DCMU-seawater once...
steps.instruction: Replace the water with DCMU-seawater once or twice a week (depending on how the anemones look: when they appear very unhappy, replace the water). Feed anemones with brine shrimp at least once a week; more feeding is better, so feed 2 – 3 times a week if possible. Importantly, after each feeding the water must be changed the next day.

steps.title: You can start screening the anemones...
steps.instruction: You can start screening the anemones under a fluorescence stereoscope after 2 – 3 weeks to check for the presence of algae. Continue incubating with DCMU until anemones appear aposymbiotic. See guidelines section for information about fluorescence filters.

steps.title: When anemones appear aposymbiotic, incubate first...
steps.instruction: When anemones appear aposymbiotic, incubate first a few of them in room-temperature seawater in the light (without DCMU) for 1 week and then check for algal presence under a fluorescence stereoscope. Tthis step ensures there are no algae still in the anemones: if any are present, they will populate quickly over the week and become detectable. If they still contain algae, continue the DCMU treatment. If they remain free of algae, they are fully aposymbiotic.

steps.title: You can start screening the anemones...
steps.instruction: You can start screening the anemones under a fluorescence stereoscope after 1.5 months to check for the presence of algae. Continue incubating in the dark until anemones appear aposymbiotic.

steps.title: When anemones appear aposymbiotic, incubate first...
steps.instruction: When anemones appear aposymbiotic, incubate first a few of them in room-temperature seawater in the light for 1 week and then check for algal presence under a fluorescence stereoscope. This step ensures there are no algae still in the anemones: if any are present, they will populate quickly over the week and become detectable. If they still contain algae, continue the dark treatment. If they remain free of algae, they are fully aposymbiotic.
